title: AAV Injection and Dorsal Root Potential Recording for Optogenetic Modulation
author: Kosugi, Sidikejiang, et al.

steps.title: Preparation for viral injection
steps.instruction:
Timing: >1 week before (for steps 1–3)
Timing: >1 day before (for step 4)

steps.title: Preparation for in vivo dorsal root recording

Timing: 5 h
Timing: 4 weeks after steps 1–28

steps.title: by dissecting the erector spinae muscles (Figure 6B).Note
steps.instruction: by dissecting the erector spinae muscles (Figure 6B).Note: Segmental levels are determined based on vertebral mobility. Vertebrae below S1 lack intervertebral motion and are thus considered immobile.63.Expose the articular processes using fine scissors, bone curette, and micro curette (Figure 6C).64.Perform a laminectomy from the 1st lumbar vertebra (L

steps.title: to S1 (Figure 6D).Note
steps.instruction: to S1 (Figure 6D).Note: Holding the vertebral body with a toothed forceps and creating sufficient space while maintaining spinal extension allows safe insertion of a bone rongeur without risking damage to the spinal cord.65.Expose the 4th (L

steps.title: lumbar DRGs (Figure 6E).CRITICAL
steps.instruction: lumbar DRGs (Figure 6E).CRITICAL: The region around the DRG is particularly prone to bleeding, which can obstruct light delivery and prevent the recording of responses to DRG irradiation. Sufficient lateral exposure is recommended to expand the surgical site such that blood pooling over the DRG is minimized.66.Begin nerve exposure. Make a 15 mm incision on the lateral thigh skin and identify the sciatic nerve between the vastus lateralis and the biceps femoris muscles (Figure 6I).67.Expose and isolate the sciatic nerve approximately 20 mm from the surrounding tissue using the glass rod with a bent tip (Figures 6J and 6K).68.Fix the animal in the stereotaxic frame.Note: Make sure the endotracheal tube and the intravenous catheter do not become dislodged while securing the animal in the stereotaxic frame. After securing the fixation, check the ventilation status again. It is recommended to temporarily remove the electrocardiogram, oxygen saturation, and rectal temperature probes.69.Suspend the limbs with the stereotaxic frame to keep the body and limbs elevated.70.Clamp both the upper thoracic spine and the base of the tail to immobilize the animal.71.Raise the skin and muscle around the exposed DRG and nerve and tie them to form a pool filled with mineral oil to protect the exposed DRG and nerve from drying out.Note: Warm the mineral oil to 36–40°C before use, and periodically check the temperature and volume, as both may decrease over time.72.Switch the anesthesia to intravenous administration of thiopental sodium (10 mg/kg).73.Administer rocuronium bromide intravenously (10 mg/kg) to achieve neuromuscular blockade.74.Open the dura with a sharp needle.75.Identify the L4 and L5 dorsal roots and separate them from the surrounding root using the glass rod with a bent tip (Figure 6F).Note: To prevent drying out, perform this procedure in the mineral oil.

description: The protocol below describes the specific steps for imparting optogenetic capabilities to modulate peripheral sensory nerve activity. Optogenetics is the combination of genetic and optical methods to control neuronal activity using light-sensitive proteins, known as opsins.2,3 Depending on the type of opsin, light irradiation can depolarize or hyperpolarize the neuronal membrane potential, thereby enabling excitation or inhibition of neural activity. Due to the heterogeneity of peripheral sensory nerves, it is crucial to parse out the contributions of functional types of sensory afferents, which necessitates the use of electrophysiological methods. Compound action potentials recorded from the dorsal root of the spinal cord (i.e., dorsal root potentials) provide a reliable electrophysiological readout for assessing both the number and functional type of modulated afferent fibers.4,5,6,7 Here, we provide a comprehensive procedure for imparting optogenetic capabilites to peripheral sensory nerves using an adeno-associated virus (AAV) vector, along with methods for precisely evaluating its effects. Specifically, we describe the injection of AAV into the sciatic nerve and the subsequent recording of dorsal root potentials at the lumbar level in male Wistar rats. This protocol can be readily adapted for other nerves and animal models, such as the common marmoset. For AAV injection procedures in marmosets, see Kudo et al.8
